ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301487 covers oyster as a food item; no specific raw-served restaurant portion code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226888001 refers to oyster as a substance; no raw-served restaurant portion concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of raw oysters at this restaurant is a minimum of 4 pieces, estimated at approximately 200 g total edible weight (approximately 50 g per oyster including liquor).

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, eastern, wild, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175159)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw eastern oysters used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent raw edible flesh per 100 g. The restaurant item is served raw with no cooking, making this a close match. Species may differ (Pacific vs eastern oysters are nutritionally similar for macro purposes); values are adapted accordingly. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated raw oyster entry; USDA SR Legacy is the highest-priority approved source available for this food.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal) / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for raw oysters exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Oysters are a low-carbohydrate shellfish (3.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g, predominantly glycogen). Shellfish and seafood are consistently classified as low-GI foods in peer-reviewed literature. A GI of approximately 5 is assigned by analogy with other low-carbohydrate shellfish and seafood items documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 7.8 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.39, rounded to 0.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (211 mg per 100 g; approximately 422 mg per 200 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A minimum 4-piece serving of raw oysters contributes approximately 422 mg sodium, representing roughly 18% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients on sodium-restricted diets should account for this contribution and avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as soy sauce or mignonette.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~5) and low carbohydrate content (3.9 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw oysters have a very low glycemic index and minimal glycemic load per serving, making them a suitable protein- and micronutrient-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the high zinc content (39.3 mg per 100 g) may additionally support insulin metabolism.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Favourable omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid profile and low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw oysters provide a beneficial lipid profile with low saturated fat and a meaningful proportion of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids, supporting cardiovascular health; however, the moderate sodium content warrants attention in patients with heart failure or sodium-sensitive hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw oysters (Hàu Sống) are live bivalve molluscs served chilled and uncooked, typically presented on the half shell with their natural liquor retained. At Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant in Singapore, they are offered as a minimum order of 4 pieces, reflecting a Vietnamese-style raw seafood presentation common in Vietnamese cuisine. Oysters are harvested from coastal or farmed marine environments and are consumed without cooking to preserve their briny, oceanic flavour and delicate, creamy texture. Nutritionally, raw oysters are a low-calorie, high-protein food providing approximately 68 kcal and 7.1 g protein per 100 g. They are exceptionally rich in zinc (39.3 mg per 100 g), making them one of the most concentrated dietary sources of this mineral, which is critical for immune function, wound healing, and insulin metabolism. They also provide significant vitamin B12 (8.75 mcg per 100 g), selenium (63.7 mcg per 100 g), and iron (5.1 mg per 100 g). The carbohydrate content is low (3.9 g per 100 g, primarily glycogen), resulting in a very low glycemic index and negligible glycemic load, making raw oysters suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content (211 mg per 100 g) is moderate and should be considered by individuals with hypertension. Raw oysters are a shellfish allergen and are not suitable for individuals with mollusc or shellfish allergies. They are naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. In Singapore's food scene, raw oysters appear on menus of Vietnamese restaurants and seafood establishments, often served with condiments such as lemon juice, Tabasco, or Vietnamese dipping sauces.
common_user_queries: How many calories in raw oysters?
What nutrients are in raw oysters?
Is Hàu Sống safe to eat?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy for raw eastern oysters (FDC ID 175159), which is an approved primary source. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a dedicated raw oyster entry. The USDA data is for eastern oysters; the restaurant may serve Pacific oysters, which are nutritionally similar but not identical. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature as no direct GI measurement for raw oysters exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The food name 'Hàu Sống (Min 4pcs)' is Vietnamese for 'raw oysters, minimum 4 pieces'. The canonical English name has been set to 'Raw Oysters (Minimum 4 Pieces)'. 2. Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 175159 (raw eastern oysters per 100 g edible portion). Pacific oysters (Crassostrea gigas), which are more common in Southeast Asian markets, have a similar but not identical nutritional profile; macro values are comparable. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; oysters contain no industrially produced trans fats. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; oyster carbohydrate is primarily glycogen, not free sugars. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; oysters are animal-derived and contain no dietary fibre. 4. GI is set to 5 (analogous estimate, low category) based on the very low carbohydrate content of oysters and peer-reviewed classification of shellfish as low-GI foods (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). No direct GI measurement for oysters exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (5 × 7.8) / 100 = 0.4. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, all B vitamins, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy and are numeric values, not sentinel -1 values. 6. Halal status is marked false as oysters are considered non-halal by many Islamic scholars (shellfish ruling varies by madhab); this should be verified for specific dietary guidance. 7. The serving size of 200 g is an estimate based on 4 oysters at approximately 50 g each (edible flesh plus liquor); actual weight may vary by oyster size and species. 8. Price SGD 36.00 is preserved from the input record for reference only and is not used in nutritional calculations.
